refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure and report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from the combined behavior of multiple FRs and design choices, not a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution provides a mechanism (sensor integration, data logging, reporting interface) to capture energy_used and ton_moved over the evaluation period, and that reported values can be independently validated against ground truth measurements.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer total work output (mass moved or equivalent productivity metric) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall calculate and report the energy efficiency ratio (energy per unit work output) at defined intervals.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors with local data aggregation; stream normalized metrics to central repository; compute and report energy_per_ton continuously with periodic validation
DP3: Establish energy accounting via fuel consumption logs and work tickets (manual or semi-automated); reconcile at period end; calculate energy_per_ton from aggregated records

DP4: Deploy dual measurement streams—energy via metered supply monitoring; work output via independent weighing/counting infrastructure—with asynchronous reconciliation and acceptance-criteria validation at reporting boundary
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Deploy dual measurement streams—energy via metered supply monitoring; work output via independent weighing/counting infrastructure—with asynchronous reconciliation and acceptance-criteria validation at reporting boundary

potential_sources: Energy measurement and work-output measurement remain logically independent (low coupling): either can be upgraded or replaced without forcing redesign of the other
Reconciliation logic is decoupled from sensor selection: the acceptance-criteria validation (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is a pure computation, not tied to specific instrument types
Asynchronous reconciliation avoids hard real-time coupling: energy and tonnage data can be collected on different schedules and synchronized at reporting time
Risk: If reconciliation logic becomes tightly bound to a specific data format or protocol, coupling increases; mitigation: define schema-agnostic reconciliation interface
coupling_check.notes: DP4 avoids the tight coupling of DP2 (real-time streaming) and the manual-process fragility of DP3. DP1 is too coarse and offers no intermediate validation. DP4 supports independent evolution of measurement infrastructure and reporting logic.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source (fuel or grid) is metered or can be instrumented without major operational disruption. Risk: If metering is unavailable or prohibitively expensive, fallback to fuel-consumption logs (DP3 hybrid) may be required.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage moved) can be measured independently at delivery point with acceptable accuracy. Risk: If delivery-point measurement is infeasible (e.g., distributed drop-off), alternative work-output proxy (e.g., distance × load) must be defined.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough (days to weeks) to average transient inefficiencies and allow asynchronous reconciliation. Risk: If acceptance criteria require sub-hourly validation, DP4 may not meet latency; escalate to DP2.

Assumption: Safety plan and operational policies do not prohibit sensor installation or data logging. Risk: If worksite restrictions are severe, measurement scope may need narrowing; clarify with safety stakeholder.

Assumption: Energy_per_ton acceptance criterion (≤ E_target) is defined and achievable with current equipment. Risk: If E_target is unrealistic, DP4 will report failure; validate E_target with engineering baseline before deployment.

Assumption: Data reconciliation can tolerate minor time-lag between energy and tonnage records (hours to days). Risk: If real-time correlation is mandated, DP4 must be augmented with synchronization logic or escalated to DP2.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP4 specification to include: (1) explicit definition of energy measurement boundary (e.g., fuel tank level, grid meter, engine ECU); (2) explicit definition of work-output measurement boundary (e.g., weighbridge at exit, GPS-based distance + load, manual tally); (3) reconciliation algorithm (e.g., daily aggregation, tolerance bands for outliers); (4) acceptance-criteria validation logic (threshold check, reporting format, escalation path if energy_per_ton > E_target). Establish IM (Implementation Module) boundaries: separate modules for energy data collection, work-output data collection, reconciliation engine, and reporting/validation. This packaging supports independent testing, maintenance, and future sensor/protocol substitution without architectural rework.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement orchestration, dual-stream data acquisition coordination, asynchronous reconciliation logic, and acceptance-criteria validation engine for energy and work-output metrics

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit reconciled energy and work-output measurements, validation status, and acceptance-criteria results to external monitoring/reporting systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive task parameters, acceptance criteria thresholds, and measurement configuration directives from task orchestration layer

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Coordinate asynchronous data streams from metered supply monitoring (energy) and independent weighing/counting infrastructure (work output) via network-based sensor integration

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: NGHE Solution is the natural home for measurement orchestration, dual-stream coordination, and reconciliation logic. Energy metering and work-output counting are core to NGHE's mission. Asynchronous reconciliation and acceptance-criteria validation are solution-level concerns. Minor diffusion: Telemetry export and Tasking input are external dependencies, but these are clean interface boundaries.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical platform, not a measurement/validation orchestrator. Energy metering and work-output counting are NGHE concerns, not vehicle-level. Reconciliation logic would be misplaced in vehicle firmware. High diffusion: measurement streams, validation, and reporting would leak into teleoperation and safety services.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a measurement reconciliation system. Dual-stream coordination and acceptance-criteria validation are orthogonal to remote operation. High diffusion: measurement logic would contaminate command/control pathways and require coupling to safety and vehicle subsystems.

Weak fit: Safety Services may validate safety-critical thresholds, but acceptance-criteria validation for energy/work-output metrics is a measurement/reporting concern, not a safety concern. Asynchronous reconciliation is not a safety function. Moderate diffusion: measurement streams and energy/work-output logic would diffuse into NGHE and Vehicle.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the authoritative owner of dual-stream measurement orchestration because: (1) energy metering and work-output counting are core NGHE observables; (2) asynchronous reconciliation is a solution-level data-fusion concern; (3) acceptance-criteria validation is a reporting/quality-gate function that belongs at the solution level; (4) all measurement streams and validation logic can be encapsulated within NGHE without leaking into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services; (5) external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link) cleanly separate NGHE measurement logic from downstream consumers and upstream task directives.
containment_risks: Risk: If metered supply monitoring is physically embedded in Vehicle hardware, the energy stream acquisition may require Vehicle-level driver code. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns the reconciliation and validation logic; Vehicle provides raw energy telemetry via Network Link interface.

Risk: If acceptance criteria are safety-critical (e.g., energy limits for hazard prevention), Safety Services may need to validate thresholds independently. Mitigation: NGHE Solution performs measurement validation; Safety Services consumes validated results via Telemetry and enforces safety interlocks independently.

Risk: Asynchronous reconciliation may introduce latency in reporting. If real-time acceptance decisions are required, NGHE Solution must buffer and prioritize streams. Mitigation: Define reconciliation timeout and fallback logic in DP; ensure Tasking interface can communicate urgency.

Risk: Dual-stream infrastructure (metering + weighing/counting) may be geographically distributed across Vehicle and external systems. Mitigation: NGHE Solution coordinates via Network Link; each stream is acquired independently and transmitted asynchronously.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly addresses the FR by specifying a dual-stream measurement architecture (energy via metered supply, work output via independent weighing/counting) with asynchronous reconciliation and acceptance-criteria validation. The DP is concrete, testable, and logically sound for enabling the required energy-per-unit-work measurement and reporting capability.

diffusion_risks: Energy stream acquisition: If metered supply monitoring driver code is embedded in Vehicle firmware rather than abstracted as a Network Link telemetry feed, reconciliation logic in NGHE Solution will be tightly coupled to Vehicle implementation details. Mitigation: enforce Vehicle->NGHE data flow via Network Link interface only; Vehicle owns sensor driver, NGHE owns reconciliation.

Work-output stream acquisition: If weighing/counting infrastructure is partially owned by external systems (e.g., third-party load cells, conveyor counters), NGHE Solution must coordinate asynchronous ingestion without owning the physical sensors. Risk of latency variance and missing data. Mitigation: define Network Link protocol for stream synchronization and timeout handling; NGHE owns reconciliation logic, not sensor hardware.

Acceptance-criteria validation: If Safety Services independently validates energy thresholds for hazard prevention (e.g., energy limits to prevent equipment damage), there is a risk of dual validation logic. Mitigation: NGHE Solution performs measurement validation against acceptance criteria; Safety Services consumes validated results and enforces safety interlocks independently. Clear separation of concerns via Telemetry interface.

Asynchronous reconciliation latency: If real-time acceptance decisions are required by Tasking layer, buffering and timeout logic in NGHE Solution may introduce unacceptable delays. Mitigation: define reconciliation SLA in DP; Tasking interface must communicate urgency and fallback behavior (e.g., reject task if reconciliation timeout exceeded).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct IM owner with 0.92 containment score. Dual-stream coordination, asynchronous reconciliation, and acceptance-criteria validation are cohesive measurement/reporting concerns that belong at the solution level. Energy metering and work-output counting are core NGHE observables. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are correctly excluded (0.35–0.40 containment scores, 0.60–0.72 diffusion scores). External interfaces (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link inout) cleanly separate NGHE measurement logic from upstream task directives and downstream consumers. Minor diffusion risk exists only at data acquisition boundaries (Vehicle energy driver, external work-output sensors), but these are mitigated by treating them as external data sources via Network Link, not as NGHE-owned modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved ≤ E_target' is directly measurable via NGHE Solution's dual-stream architecture. Energy is acquired from metered supply monitoring (quantifiable in kWh or joules); work output is acquired from independent weighing/counting infrastructure (quantifiable in tons or units moved). Asynchronous reconciliation ensures both streams are aligned before computing the ratio. Validation logic can be unit-tested and integration-tested against known energy/work datasets. Reporting via Telemetry interface enables external verification and audit trails.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of measurement orchestration, reconciliation, and acceptance-criteria validation. Ownership boundaries are well-defined: NGHE owns DP logic; Vehicle owns energy sensor drivers (abstracted via Network Link); external systems own work-output sensors (abstracted via Network Link); Tasking layer owns task parameters and acceptance-criteria thresholds (input via Tasking interface); Telemetry consumers own downstream reporting and compliance documentation. No ambiguity in module responsibility. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that inform acceptance-criteria thresholds (input via Tasking interface), not NGHE-owned logic.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define Network Link protocol for dual-stream data transmission: specify message format, timestamp precision, latency bounds, and retry/timeout behavior for asynchronous reconciliation. This clarifies the Vehicle->NGHE and external-sensor->NGHE data contracts.

Add reconciliation SLA to DP4: specify maximum acceptable latency between energy and work-output stream arrival, and define fallback behavior (e.g., reject measurement, flag as incomplete, or use last-known-good reconciliation). This mitigates asynchronous latency risk.

Clarify ownership of acceptance-criteria thresholds: specify whether E_target is static (defined at design time) or dynamic (updated per task via Tasking interface). If dynamic, define validation logic in NGHE Solution to reject out-of-range thresholds.

Define reconciliation failure modes: specify behavior when energy or work-output stream is missing, delayed, or corrupted. Should NGHE Solution halt reporting, use interpolation, or escalate to Tasking layer? Add error handling to DP4.

Specify Telemetry output schema: include reconciled energy, work output, computed energy_per_ton ratio, validation status (pass/fail), timestamp, and any reconciliation warnings. This enables external audit and compliance verification.

Confirm that Vehicle energy metering is non-invasive (e.g., via utility meter or power supply monitoring) and does not require Vehicle firmware changes. If Vehicle firmware changes are needed, document as a Vehicle DP dependency and ensure NGHE Solution treats energy as an external telemetry feed, not an owned subsystem.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is concrete, testable, and logically sound; the IM (NGHE Solution) is correctly scoped with 0.92 containment and clear ownership boundaries. However, the recommended_revisions expose critical sub-concerns that must be decomposed to operationalize the dual-stream architecture and mitigate asynchronous reconciliation risks. Given high safety criticality, explicit sub-FRs for reconciliation SLA, failure-mode handling, and Network Link protocol are necessary to ensure the DP can be reliably implemented and verified. Decomposition is justified to address these concerns at the next level rather than deferring them to detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Acquire and timestamp energy consumption data from metered supply with specified precision and latency bounds.
FR4.2: Acquire and timestamp work-output data (weight/count) from independent infrastructure with specified precision and latency bounds.
FR4.3: Reconcile dual-stream data asynchronously, ensuring temporal alignment within defined SLA and handling stream delays/gaps.
FR4.4: Validate reconciled energy-per-unit-work ratio against acceptance criteria (E_target) and report pass/fail status with diagnostic detail.
FR4.5: Define and enforce Network Link protocol for energy and work-output stream transmission, including message format, retry logic, and timeout behavior.
FR4.6: Handle reconciliation failure modes (missing/delayed/corrupted streams) with explicit fallback behavior and escalation to Tasking layer.
FR4.7: Generate and transmit Telemetry output schema (reconciled energy, work output, ratio, validation status, timestamp, warnings) for external audit and compliance verification.

revise.why: DP4 is logically sound but lacks operational detail on reconciliation SLA, failure-mode handling, and Network Link protocol. Revise DP4 to explicitly reference sub-DP solutions for each sub-FR (e.g., 'DP4.3: Asynchronous reconciliation with configurable timeout and last-known-good fallback') to ensure the decomposition is traceable and implementable. This revision strengthens the DP-IM link by clarifying what NGHE Solution must own vs. what is external.

assumptions: Vehicle energy metering is non-invasive (utility meter or power supply monitoring) and does not require Vehicle firmware changes; if changes are needed, they are documented as a Vehicle DP dependency and NGHE treats energy as an external telemetry feed.
Work-output sensors (load cells, conveyor counters) are owned by external systems; NGHE Solution coordinates ingestion via Network Link without owning the physical hardware.
Acceptance-criteria thresholds (E_target) are either static (design-time) or dynamic (per-task via Tasking interface); if dynamic, NGHE Solution validates threshold ranges.
Reconciliation SLA (maximum acceptable latency between energy and work-output stream arrival) is defined and communicated to Tasking layer to inform task acceptance decisions.

Safety Services independently validates energy thresholds for hazard prevention; NGHE Solution performs measurement validation against acceptance criteria and Safety Services consumes validated results via Telemetry interface.
open_risks: Asynchronous reconciliation latency: If real-time acceptance decisions are required by Tasking layer, buffering and timeout logic may introduce unacceptable delays. Mitigation: define reconciliation SLA in sub-DP4.3 and communicate to Tasking interface; Tasking layer must handle timeout fallback behavior.

Energy stream acquisition coupling: If metered supply monitoring driver code is embedded in Vehicle firmware rather than abstracted as Network Link telemetry feed, reconciliation logic in NGHE Solution will be tightly coupled to Vehicle implementation. Mitigation: enforce Vehicle->NGHE data flow via Network Link interface only; Vehicle owns sensor driver, NGHE owns reconciliation.

Work-output stream latency variance: If weighing/counting infrastructure is partially owned by external systems, NGHE Solution must coordinate asynchronous ingestion without owning the physical sensors. Risk of latency variance and missing data. Mitigation: define Network Link protocol for stream synchronization and timeout handling in sub-DP4.5.

Dual validation logic: If Safety Services independently validates energy thresholds for hazard prevention, there is a risk of dual validation logic. Mitigation: NGHE Solution performs measurement validation against acceptance criteria; Safety Services consumes validated results and enforces safety interlocks independently via Telemetry interface.

Reconciliation failure modes: Behavior when energy or work-output stream is missing, delayed, or corrupted is not yet specified. Risk of silent failures or inconsistent fallback behavior. Mitigation: define explicit failure-mode handling in sub-DP4.6 with escalation to Tasking layer.
evidence_needed: Network Link protocol specification: message format, timestamp precision (e.g., millisecond or microsecond), latency bounds (e.g., <100ms for energy, <500ms for work-output), and retry/timeout behavior for asynchronous reconciliation.

Reconciliation SLA definition: maximum acceptable latency between energy and work-output stream arrival (e.g., <1 second), and fallback behavior (e.g., reject measurement, flag as incomplete, or use last-known-good reconciliation).
Acceptance-criteria threshold ownership: specification of whether E_target is static (design-time) or dynamic (per-task via Tasking interface), and validation logic to reject out-of-range thresholds.
Failure-mode handling specification: explicit behavior when energy or work-output stream is missing, delayed, or corrupted (e.g., halt reporting, use interpolation, escalate to Tasking layer).
Telemetry output schema: detailed specification including reconciled energy, work output, computed energy_per_ton ratio, validation status (pass/fail), timestamp, and reconciliation warnings.

Vehicle energy metering non-invasiveness confirmation: documentation that Vehicle energy metering does not require Vehicle firmware changes, or if changes are needed, they are documented as a Vehicle DP dependency.
Integration test plan: test cases for dual-stream reconciliation with known energy/work datasets, latency variance scenarios, and failure-mode handling (missing/delayed/corrupted streams).
